--- SOURCE 1: 26 The Phenomenological Approach - Oxford Academic ---
URL: https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/27976/chapter/211633335

SUMMARY:
Classic phenomenology—primarily the work of Husserl, Heidegger, Max Scheler, Edith Stein, Alfred Schutz, Jean-Paul Sartre, and Maurice Merleau-Ponty —proposed a very broad program of research that included essential analyses of all the central aspects of human existence: intentionality, consciousness, perception,

--- SOURCE 5: Phenomenology (philosophy) - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)

SUMMARY:
Phenomenology is contrasted with phenomenalism, which reduces mental states and physical objects to complexes of sensations "Sensation (psychology)"),#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisette2011-5) and with psychologism, which treats logical truths or epistemological principles as the products of human psychology.#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavidson1988-6) In particular, transcendental phenomenology, as outlined by Edmund Husserl, aims to arrive at an objective understanding of the world via the discovery of universal logical structures in human subjective experience.#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolowski1999-1) According to phenomenologist Maurice Natanson, "The radicality of the phenomenological method is both continuous and discontinuous with philosophy's general effort to subject experience to fundamental, critical scrutiny: to take nothing for granted and to show the warranty for what we claim to know."#cite_note-FOOTNOTENatanson197363-30) Husserl says that the suspension of belief in what is ordinarily taken for granted or inferred by conjecture diminishes the power of what is customarily embraced as objective reality.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Philosophical method and schools of philosophy

For other uses, see [Phenomenology (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(disambiguation) "Phenomenology (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Phenomenalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism").

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Edmund_Husserl_1910s.jpg/250px-Edmund_Husserl_1910s.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Edmund_Husserl_1910s.jpg)

[Edmund Husserl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl")

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Heidegger_2_%281960%29.jpg/250px-Heidegger_2_%281960%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Heidegger_2_(1960).jpg)

[Martin Heidegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger")

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Sartre_1967_crop.jpg/250px-Sartre_1967_crop.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sartre_1967_crop.jpg)

[Jean-Paul Sartre](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Paul_Sartre "Jean-Paul Sartre")

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Maurice_Merleau-Ponty.jpg/120px-Maurice_Merleau-Ponty.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Maurice_Merleau-Ponty.jpg)

[Maurice Merleau-Ponty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maurice_Merleau-Ponty "Maurice Merleau-Ponty")

**Phenomenology** is a philosophical study and movement largely associated with the early 20th century that seeks to [objectively](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjectivity_and_objectivity_(philosophy) "Subjectivity and objectivity (philosophy)") investigate the nature of subjective, [conscious](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consciousness "Consciousness") experience. It attempts to describe the universal features of consciousness while avoiding assumptions about the external world, aiming to describe [phenomena](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenon "Phenomenon") as they appear, and to explore the meaning and significance of [lived experience](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lived_experience "Lived experience").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolowski1999-1)

This approach, while philosophical, has found many applications in [qualitative research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qualitative_research "Qualitative research") across different scientific disciplines, especially in the [social sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_science "Social science"), [humanities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(psychology) "Phenomenology (psychology)"), and [cognitive science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_science "Cognitive science"), but also in fields as diverse as [health sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_sciences "Health sciences"),[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavidsen2011-2)[architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(architecture) "Phenomenology (architecture)"),[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeamon2018-3) and [human-computer interaction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human%E2%80%93computer_interaction "Human–computer interaction"),[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTECilesiz2011-4) among many others. The application of phenomenology in these fields aims to gain a deeper understanding of subjective experience, rather than focusing on [behavior](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behaviorism "Behaviorism").

Phenomenology is contrasted with [phenomenalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenalism "Phenomenalism"), which reduces [mental states](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mental_state "Mental state") and physical objects to complexes of [sensations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensation_(psychology) "Sensation (psychology)"),[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisette2011-5) and with [psychologism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychologism "Psychologism"), which treats [logical truths](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logical_truth "Logical truth") or [epistemological](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epistemology "Epistemology") principles as the products of human psychology.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavidson1988-6) In particular, [transcendental phenomenology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transcendental_phenomenology "Transcendental phenomenology"), as outlined by [Edmund Husserl](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmund_Husserl "Edmund Husserl"), aims to arrive at an objective understanding of the world via the discovery of universal logical structures in human subjective experience.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESokolowski1999-1)

There are important differences in the ways that different branches of phenomenology approach [subjectivity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subjectivity "Subjectivity"). For example, according to [Martin Heidegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger"), truths are contextually situated and dependent on the [historical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History "History"), [cultural](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culture "Culture"), and [social context](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_context "Social context") in which they emerge. Other types include [hermeneutic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics"), [genetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Genetic_phenomenology "Genetic phenomenology"), and [embodied](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Embodied_cognition "Embodied cognition") phenomenology. All these different branches of phenomenology may be seen as representing different philosophies despite sharing the common foundational approach of phenomenological inquiry; that is, investigating things just as they appear, independent of any particular theoretical framework.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZahavi2018-7)

Etymology
---------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Phenomenology_(philosophy)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term _phenomenology_ derives from the [Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") φαινόμενον, _phainómenon_ ("that which appears") and λόγος, _lógos_ ("study"). It entered the English language around the turn of the 18th century and first appeared in direct connection to Husserl's philosophy in a 1907 article in _[The Philosophical Review](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Philosophical\_Review "The Philosophical Review")_.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-8)

In philosophy, "phenomenology" refers to the tradition inaugurated by Edmund Husserl at the beginning of the 20th century.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2023Introduction-9) The term, however, had been used in different senses in other philosophy texts since the 18th century. These include those by [Johann Heinrich Lambert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johann_Heinrich_Lambert "Johann Heinrich Lambert") (1728–1777), [Immanuel Kant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") (1724–1804), [G. W. F. Hegel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G._W._F._Hegel "G. W. F. Hegel") (1770–1831), and [Carl Stumpf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Stumpf "Carl Stumpf") (1848–1936), among others.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinelli201523%E2%80%9343-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran20006%E2%80%937-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2022%C2%A73-12)

It was, however, the usage of [Franz Brentano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franz_Brentano "Franz Brentano") (and, as he later acknowledged, [Ernst Mach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach")[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisette2011-5)) that would prove definitive for Husserl.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran20007-13) From Brentano, Husserl took the conviction that philosophy must commit itself to _description_ of what is "given in direct 'self-evidence'."[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran20007%E2%80%938-14)

Central to Brentano's phenomenological project was his theory of [intentionality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intentionality "Intentionality"), which he developed from his reading of [Aristotle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")'s _[On the Soul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On\_the\_Soul "On the Soul")_.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran20008-15) According to the phenomenological tradition, "the central structure of an experience is its intentionality, it being directed _towards_ something, as it is an experience _of_ or _about_ some object."[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2022Introduction,_emphasis_added-16) Also, on this theory, every intentional act is implicitly accompanied by a secondary, pre-reflective awareness of the act as one's own.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoran20008%E2%80%939-17)

Overview
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Phenomenology_(philosophy)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

Phenomenology proceeds systematically, but it does not attempt to study consciousness from the perspective of clinical psychology or neurology. Instead, it seeks to determine the essential properties and structures of experience.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMenonSinhaSreekantan2014172-18) Phenomenology is not a matter of individual introspection: _a subjective account of experience_, which is the topic of psychology, must be distinguished from _an account of subjective experience_, which is the topic of phenomenology.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagherZahavi202121-19) Its topic is not "mental states", but "worldly things considered in a certain way".[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2023%C2%A72.a-20)

Phenomenology is a direct reaction to the [psychologism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychologism "Psychologism") and [physicalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physicalism "Physicalism") of Husserl's time.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHusserl1970part_III,_%C2%A757-21) It takes as its point of departure the question of how objectivity is possible at all when the experience of the world and its objects is thoroughly subjective.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagherZahavi202125-22)

So far from being a form of subjectivism, phenomenologists argue that the scientific ideal of a purely objective third-person is a fantasy and falsity. The perspective and presuppositions of the scientist must be articulated and taken into account in the design of the experiment and the interpretation of its results. Inasmuch as phenomenology is able to accomplish this, it can help to improve the quality of empirical scientific research.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagherZahavi2021chapter_2-23)

Notwithstanding of the field's internal diversity, [Shaun Gallagher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaun_Gallagher "Shaun Gallagher") and [Dan Zahavi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dan_Zahavi "Dan Zahavi") argue that the phenomenological method is composed of four basic steps: the [epoché](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epoch%C3%A9 "Epoché"), the phenomenological reduction, the eidetic variation, and intersubjective corroboration.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenomenology_(philosophy)#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGallagherZahavi202130-24)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Husserl, the founder of phenomenology, emphasized intentionality, consciousness, and the epoché, aiming to reveal the structures of experience and establish a scientific philosophy.
Heidegger expanded phenomenology to existential themes, focusing on being, temporality, and the situatedness of human experience in a historical and cultural context.
Merleau-Ponty highlighted embodied perception, asserting that our bodily experience shapes our understanding of the world, bridging the gap between subjectivity and objectivity.
Schutz, a sociologist, integrated phenomenology with social sciences, exploring how individuals construct meaning in everyday life through shared experiences and intersubjectivity.
